Dear Mr. KyIe:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regard-
ing the duties and responsibi tities of Mr. David Bing, a member of
the board of directors of the Livermore Area Recreation and Park
District, 
under the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the nAct").1 Your letter does not request
advice regarding a specj-f lc decj.sion before the agency but rather
seeks greneral guidance. Moreover, althougth you have stated that
the boird of directors authorized your request for advice, you
have not stated in your Letter that Mr. Bing has authorized you to
seek advice on his Lehal-f . Accordingly, sre are treating your
request as one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
l-8i29 (c) (copy enclosed).2
we do not provide advice concerning provisions of lal' other
than the Act. iou uray wish to consult with the Attorney General's
office regarding other provisions of faw such as section 1090
which proiibj-ts governrnent officials fron having an interest in
contracts with their agencies.
OUESTIONS
1. Does the Act require Mr. Binq to refrain from submitting
bids for the construction of rrturn keyrr parks, and if he is the
successful bidder, from contracting with any developer who might
1' 
covernment code secti.ons 81ooo-91015. All- statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations Section
l-8ooo, et ses. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
provide the requestor with the
or formal $/ritten advice. (sec-
)
CalifOrnia
Fair POlitical
Practices Cornrnission
Michael- E. Kyle
Attorney at Las/
l- 17 1 I'lurrieta Boulevard
Livermore, CA 9455O-4L93
2 rnforrnaL assistance does not
imrnunity provj-ded by an opinion
tion 831L4; Regulation 18329 (c)
July 3. L99o
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
our File No, I-9 0 -3 05
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento cA 958O4-O8O7 o (916) 322.5660


File No. I-9 o-3 05
Page 2
propose a project to be built within the geographical boundarj'es
of the district?
2. Does the Act prevent Mr. Bing fron participating in
governmental decisions related to the construction of Itturn keyrl
parks?
CONCLUSIONS
1. The Act does not require l'!r. Bing to refrain frorn submit-
ting bids for the construction of rtturn keyrt parks, nor does the
Act prevent Mr. Bing from contracting with any developer who might
prop-ose a project ta be built nrithin the geographical boundaries
of the district.
2. The Act requires Mr. Bing to disqualify hinself fron
participating in deCisions which will have a material financial
Ettect on any source of incone of $250 or more in the 12 nonths
preceding the decision, including developers and the company of
ini"h n"-i= a rnajor shareholder. Additionally, the rrnexus, provi-
sions of the Act may require Mr. Bing to disqualify hinself fron
participating in de-isions to accept a trturn keytr park for the
Lonstrultion of which he has been the successful bidder.
FACTS
The Livermore Area Recreation and Park District 
(the
district) 
is a special district 
formed pursuant to the provisions
of the Public Resources code. The district 
is governed by a five-
member elected board of directors. 
The district 
is responsible
for the construction and maintenance of parks and recreational
facj,lities and for providing recreational activities in the
Livermore area.
Pursuant to the terms of a joint powers aqreement (JPA)
executed in 1970, the city of Livermore and the district 
share
responsibility for providing parks and recreation services for the
cit-izens of Livermoie. The city owns most of the park sites
vrithin its boundaries, and the responsibility for their develop-
rnent and maintenance is delegated to the district. 
The district
owns several park sites in its own name including a 365-acre
nature park outside the city Lirnits. The city acquires parks
through purchases, deveJ.oper dedications, and gifts. 
Under the
terms of the JPA, any funds cotlected by the city in the form of
developer in lieu fees3 are directty transferable to the district
for the construction of parks.
3 Th" legislative body of a city or county nay, by ordinance,
require the dedication of land or inpose a requirement of the pay-
ment of fees in lieu thereof, or a conbination of both, for
neighborhood and comnunity park or recreational purposes as a
condition to the approval of a tentative nap or parcel nap. (Sec-
t-ior. 65477 . )


ヽ
、
、
　
一
　
一
一
”
　
　
．
　
＾


File No. I-9 0-3 05
Page 3
Mr. Bing is a member of the board of directors of the
Livermore Area Recreation and Park District. 
I'1r. Bing also is a
principal stockhoLder in White oak Landscape, a California
corporation. White oak Landscape has a construction division and
a maintenance division. 
white oak Landscape has never directly
contracted for construction or maintenance work with the district.
Hovrever, the corporation performs lrork for developers j.n "turn
key'r parks built by developers and subsequently dedicated to the
public entity having jurisdiction for the development of the
project and park in question. Usually, title to the park is
turned over to the pubtic entity through the dedication process-
whenever white oak Landscape is the successful, bidder for a
project, it builds the park in accordance with pre-drawn
specifications. 
White Oak LandscaPe does not do any projects for
pultic entities or developers on a "design-buildrt basis.
whenever a deveLoper builds and dedicates a park within the
Liverrnore city lirnits, the district 
is the responsibLe lead
agency. The district, 
along with the city's design review commit-
tee, lngineering department, planning staff and building inspec-
tion department, has jurisdiction for approval or rejection of
plans submj,tted and built by a developer. Pursuant to the terms
of tfre JPA, it is possible for district staff to have input in the
process at several stages.
Initially, 
as part of the tract maP approval- process, tenta-
tive maps are delivered to the district for review and approval- by
the boaid of directors. 
In the case of residentj-a1 projects, the
board usuall-y reconmends whether the city shouLd require dedica-
tj-on of park land or, in the alternative, the payment of in lieu
fees. The city is not bound by the district's 
recommendation but
generally pays deference to its recommendations. where the
ionstruction of a park is ca11ed for, district staff review the
developer's plans and subnittals for conformance $/ith the
district's 
naster plan and for the appropriateness of the park
location and the eguipnent proposed for the park. As the park is
being bui1t, district 
staff works with city staff to inspect for
confornance to the plans and specifications and to approve the
developer's sub-contractor's work. The city inspectors inspect
the water and electrical portions of the job and district 
staff
j-nspects and approves or rejects the installation of playground,
irrigation and related eguipnent.
You have asked us to assume, for purposes of our discussion,
that the only involvement of the district 
board in the process is
to render a decision on whether or not to recommend construction
of a park or receipt of in lieu fees under the park dedication
ordinance. You also have asked us to assune that at the tirne the
decision is made by the board, lrr. Bing does not know whether his
company will be subnitting a bid to the developer who is process-
ing the tentative tract map through the city approval process.
once the developer has completed the approval. process, if
construction of a park is required, white oak Landscape may subrnit


File No. I-9 0-3 05
Page 4
a bid for constructj,on of the park. In this event, the bid is
generally in excess of $1oo,ooo and Mr. Bing's compensation
exceeds $250 for any work done on the project.
ANAIYSI S
The PoliticaL Reform Act lras enacted by the peopLe of the
state of California by initiative 
in 1974. The purpose for the
disclosure and disgual if ication provisions of the Act is to ensure
that public officials, 
I^Ihether elected or appointed, perforrn their
duties in an impartial nanner, free from bias caused by their own
financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have
supported then. (section 81001(b).) In furtherance of these
obj-ctives, Section 8710o prohibits a public official 
frorn making,
paiticipating j-n or atternpting to influence any governnental deci-
sion in which he or she knoyrs or has reason to know he or she has
a financial interest. 
A menber of the board of directors of the
Livermore Area Recreation and Park District is a public official.
(Section 82048. )
An official 
has a financial interest in a governmental deci-
sion if it is reasonabl-y foreseeable that the decision will have a
naterial financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public Aenerally, on the official 
or a member of his inrnediate
fanily or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or Dore.
***
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than Ioans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
officiaL status, aggregating tr/o hundred fifty 
doJ.-
lars ($250) or more in vatue provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within 12
months prior to the tine when the decisi.on is made.
(d) Any business
official 
is a director,
ernployee, or holds any
entity in which the public
officer, partner, trustee,
position of uanagement.
***
For purposes of this section, indirect invest-
nent or j-nterest means any investnent or interest
orrned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official, 
by an agent on behalf of a pubLic of-
ficial , or by a business entity or trust in which
the official, 
the official's 
agents, spouse, and


Fi■e No. I-90-305
Page 5
dependent children own directly, indirectl-y, or
benef iciall-y a 1o-percent interest or greater.
section 87103 (a) , (c) , and (d) .
You have stated in your request for advice that ur. Bing is a
principal stockholder in l{hite Oak Landscape and that when the
company completes a project Iilr. Bing receives incorne in excess of
$zso. For purposes of our discussion we also assume that ur.
Bing's interest in the company exceeds S1,000. Under this set of
facts, Mr. Bing is required to disqualify hirnself frorn participat-
ing in any decision which would foreseeably and materially affect
hin or white oak Landscape. !rr. Bing is also required to
disqualify himself from participating in any decision which rrould
foreseeabLy and naterially affect any person (including a business
entity) who has been a source of income in excess of $250 in the 12
months preceding the decision, or any business entity in which Mr.
Bing is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds
any position of management. Disqualification is required when the
decision will affect these interests in a manner that is
distinguishable from the effect on the public generaLly.
Foreseeabi I itv
The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur. To be foresee-
able, the effects of a decision nust be more than a rnere possibil-
ity; however certainty is not required. 
(Downev cares v. Dolrney
communitv Development Com. (L987) L96 CaI. APp. 3d 983, 9A9-991-;
Witt v. l.torro$, (1977) 70 CaI. App. 3d 8L7; In re Thorner (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed). ) The Act seeks to prevent more than
actual conflicts of interest, it seeks to prevent even the appear-
ance of a possible conflict of interest. 
(witt v. UgI-E-gI{, supra,
at 823. )
White oak Landscape is engaged in the business of construc-
tion and naintenance of parks. The comPany has, in the past,
subrnitted bids to developers for the development of rrturn keyrl
projects. 
If it is reasonably foreseeable that white oak Landscape
will subrnit a bid and be alrarded the project, Mr. Bing rnust
disqualify from any decision related to a rrturn keyrr park. For
exanple, if a certain developer generaLly contracts with White oak
Landscape whenever a project requires a itturn keyrt park, it is
reasonably foreseeable that rrhen the particular developer comes
before the district 
to seek approval for a park the decision will
have a financial effect on oak Park Landscape which is 1ikely to be
the successfuL bidder for construction of the park. If it is
foreseeable that the decision rri1l have a financial effect on the
company, disqualification is required lrhen the effect will be rnate-
rial.


File No. I-9 0-3 05
Page 5
Mater ia l itv
when a source of incorne (including a business entity) of $250
or more in the 12 months preceding a decision is directly affected
by the decision, the effect of the decision is rnaterial. 
(Regula-
tion 18702.1, copy enclosed.) Thus, if a project involving a
developer who has been a source of income to Mr. Bing of S250 or
more in the 12 months preceding a decision is before the district
board, Mr. Bing must disgualify hinself fron participating in the
decision.
Additionally, if a developer who has been a source of incorne
to Mr. Bing of $250 or more in the 12 months preceding a decision
is indirectly affected by the decision, the applicable standards
for deternining nateriaLity are set forth in Regulations 18702.2
through La7o2.6 (copies enclosed).
A decision to require the deveLopnent of a park instead of
paynent of in lieu fees does not affect white oak Landscape
aiiectfy. 
Thus, the effect of the decision on the company is
indirect. 
when a decisi.on will have an indirect effect on a
financial .interest, Regulation L8702.2 provides guideJ.ines for
deterurining if the effect wilL be material. 
we have insufficient
infornation about the financial size of the company to determine
the applicable standards. Holrever, subsection (S) of Regulation
1a7o2.2, applicable to all but the largest business concerns,
provides that a decision is deemed roaterial if:
1. The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
$10,000 or more; or
2. The decision witl result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or elilri.nating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or
3. The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or tiabilities 
of
$1o,000 or more.
Regulation L87 02 .2 (q) .
Applying this regulation to the facts you have provided,' it would
appear that award of a bid for a S100,000 project Irould have a
rnlterial effect on White oak Landscape. This is so because the
$1oO,ooo tould increase the gross revenues of the company for the
4  .f there are additional facts availab■e to you that make it
reasonab■y foreseeable that the decision wi■■ have an effect on
the company′  then you must dete■.uine mater■a■ity pursuant to
Regulations ■8702.■ through ■8702.6.




File No. I-90-305
Page 7
f j.scal year in a sum which is in excess of $L0,000. Under these
circurnsfances, disqualification is required. 5
PubIic Generallv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is naterial, disqualification is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87100.) If the decision does not affect all members of
the public in the same manner, disqualification may be required
unl,ess the effect of the decisi-on on the source of incorne is the
same as the effect on a significant segTment of the public.
(Regulation 18703, copy enclosed. )
Decisions regarding turn key parks will not affect a
significant segrment of ahe public in the sane manner as such deci-
si5ns wi}l affect liIr. Bing. This is so because Mr. Bing's
financial interests may be affected by such decisj-ons- Accord-
ing1y, the public generally exception is inapplicable to your
facts .
Nexus
Additionally, l,tr. Bing will be required to disqualify hinself
if there is a "nexus" between the purpose for which he receives
incone in his private capacity and a governmental decision. 
A
nexus situatio-n exists ii tne official 
receives income to achieve
a goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or
hindered fy ttre decision. 
(Regnrlation La7o2.1(d).) You have
asked us to assume that the only invotvement of the board with
"turn keyrr parks is to deterroine whether or not to recommend
construclion of a park or receipt of in lieu fees. However, if
upon completion of the project the park wilt come before the board
for approval of the hrork performed, ltr. Bing, as a member.of the
conpany which cornpleted the project, may not participate. in. the
aeclsion. under Lhese circumstances, there is a disqualifying
nexus because Mr. Bing wouJ,d be approving in his official 
capacity
that for which he received income to accomplish in his private
capacity. Disqualification is thus requ j-red.
' This presunes that based on the financial size of white oak
Landscape, subdivision (S) of Regulation 7a7o2.2 is indeed ap-
pIicab1e. If subdivision (g) does not aPP1Y, nateriality is
deterrnined by applying the appropriate standard in Regulation
ta7 02 .2 .


FiIe No. I-9 o-3 05
Page 8
we trust this Letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
should you have any further questions regardinq this natter, do
not hesitate to call ne at (916) 322-5901.
S incerely,
Scott Hallabrin
